title: Structured Physical Training Introduction
pillar_name: Physical Literacy, Martial Arts & Human Performance

suggested.title: [Equipment] Weighted vest or small rucksack (5–15 lbs)

competence_markers.description: The student can explain progressive overload in their own words — the concept that doing more over time makes you stronger
The student maintains a training log and can show progression over weeks
The student executes 3 structured training sessions per week independently (with mentor oversight but not prompting)
The student can perform a goblet squat with a kettlebell with good form (full depth, upright torso)
The student can perform a two-hand kettlebell swing with correct hip hinge mechanics (not squatting the swing)
The student can run 1 mile without stopping
The student can ruck 2 miles with 10–15% bodyweight without significant distress
The student shows measurable improvement in at least 2 bodyweight benchmarks (push-ups, hang time, squat count) over the packet period

cross_connections.description: This packet is the direct on-ramp. The concepts of sets, reps, progressive overload, and training logs carry directly into barbell training.
A student who trains hard and eats poorly will stagnate. Nutrition and training are inseparable.
Keeping a training log and executing sessions without being prompted are IC2 skills in physical form.

content.why_this_matters:
You have been building strength through play, carrying, climbing, and work. Now you are going to add some structure. Not a lot — you are still young, and play is still your primary training. But you are going to learn what sets and reps mean, what it feels like to push yourself to a specific number, and how your body gets stronger when you ask it to do a little more each week.

This is the bridge between playing hard and training smart. By the time this packet is complete, you will understand the basic idea that makes all training work: if you do a little more than last time — one more push-up, five more seconds of hanging, ten more yards of carrying — your body adapts and gets stronger. This is called progressive overload, and it is the most important concept in strength training.
content.what_this_develops:
1. **The concept of progressive overload** — doing a little more each session to drive adaptation
2. **Basic training structure** — sets, reps, and rest periods
3. **Bodyweight training habits** — a simple, repeatable training session the student can execute independently
4. **The connection between effort and improvement** — the understanding that progress requires deliberate work
5. **Kettlebell introduction** — basic goblet squat and two-hand swing with an age-appropriate weight
6. **Endurance beyond sprints** — longer runs, rucks with light loads, sustained-effort work
content.core_content:
### Part 1: Progressive Overload — The Engine of All Strength

Your body adapts to stress. When you do push-ups, your muscles experience stress. Your body repairs them and makes them slightly stronger so that the same stress is easier next time. If you do the same number of push-ups every day forever, your body adapts to that number and stops getting stronger. You have to do more to keep improving.

This is progressive overload: systematically increasing the demand on your body over time. More reps. More weight. More distance. More time. Longer holds. The form of progress does not matter as much as the fact that progress is happening.

**Example:** You can do 8 push-ups today. Next week, you do 9. The week after, 10. The week after, 11. In a month, you can do 12. In three months, you can do 20. No single session was dramatically harder than the one before. But the accumulated progression turned 8 into 20.

### Part 2: Sets, Reps, and Rest

**Rep (repetition):** One complete movement. One push-up is one rep.
**Set:** A group of reps done without stopping. 10 push-ups is one set of 10 reps.
**Rest:** The time between sets. Usually 1–3 minutes depending on the exercise and the goal.

**A simple training session structure:**
- Warm up (5 minutes of movement — crawling, jumping, light jogging)
- Exercise 1: 3 sets of [your working number] with 1–2 minutes rest between sets
- Exercise 2: 3 sets of [your working number] with 1–2 minutes rest
- Exercise 3: 3 sets of [your working number] with 1–2 minutes rest
- Cool down (5 minutes of stretching and hanging)

This is not complicated. Three exercises, three sets each, appropriate rest. The whole session takes 20–30 minutes. The key is consistency — do this 3 times per week and track your numbers.

### Part 3: Your Training Session

Here is a simple, effective training session you can do 3 times per week:

**Exercise 1: Push-ups** — 3 sets of as many as you can do with good form. Record the number. Try to beat it next time.

**Exercise 2: Pull-up progression** — 3 sets of whatever your current level is (dead hangs for time, flexed-arm hangs, negatives, or pull-ups). Record what you did.

**Exercise 3: Squats** — 3 sets of 15–20 bodyweight squats. Full depth, heels flat, chest up.

**Exercise 4 (optional): Farmer's walk** — 2 carries of the heaviest load you can manage for 100+ yards.

Write down what you do each session. Compare to last session. Do one more rep or hold five more seconds. That is the whole system.

### Part 4: The Kettlebell — Your First Training Tool

A kettlebell is a cannonball with a handle. It is one of the most effective and versatile training tools ever created. Two exercises with a kettlebell — the goblet squat and the two-hand swing — build more functional strength and conditioning than a dozen gym machines.

**Goblet squat:** Hold the kettlebell by the horns (the sides of the handle) at chest height. Squat all the way down — hips below knees. The weight helps you balance and stay upright. Stand up. The goblet squat teaches perfect squat mechanics and builds leg strength.

**Two-hand kettlebell swing:** This is the most important kettlebell exercise. The bell starts between your legs, you hinge at the hips (like snapping a towel with your hips), and the bell swings to chest height. Your arms do not lift the bell — your hips drive it. This trains your entire posterior chain (glutes, hamstrings, back) and builds explosive hip power.

**Safety:** Learn the swing from your mentor or from a video with your mentor watching. The hip hinge is the key movement — if you do not hinge, you squat the swing, which loads your back instead of your hips. Practice the hinge without the kettlebell first: stand a foot from a wall, push your hips back until they touch the wall, stand back up. That is the hinge.

### Part 5: Endurance — Longer Efforts

Sprints build explosive power. But you also need the ability to sustain effort over longer periods.

**Rucking:** Put 10–15% of your bodyweight in a backpack and walk. Start with 1 mile. Build to 2–3 miles. Rucking builds leg strength, core endurance, cardiovascular fitness, and mental toughness simultaneously. It is the conditioning staple of every military in the world.

**Longer runs:** Run 1 mile without stopping. Then 1.5 miles. Then 2 miles. Not fast — sustainable pace. Conversation pace. The ability to run 2 miles without stopping puts you ahead of most adults.

**Sustained work:** 30–60 minutes of continuous physical work — carrying, digging, hauling, building. No breaks longer than a minute. This builds the work capacity that Weathering Storms structured training depends on.

mentor_guide.competence_evaluation:
### 1. Progressive Overload Understanding
**Developing well:** The student can explain in their own words why you need to do more over time to get stronger. They reference their training log to show progression.

**Developing slowly:** Can repeat the definition but does not apply it. Trains the same numbers every session without attempting to progress.

### 2. Training Consistency
**Developing well:** Completes 3 sessions per week with minimal prompting. Opens their training log before starting. Records everything.

**Developing slowly:** Sessions are sporadic. Forgets to log. Needs constant reminding to train.

### 3. Kettlebell Technique
**Developing well:** Goblet squat reaches full depth with upright torso. Swing uses clear hip hinge — hips snap forward, arms are passive. Bell reaches chest height from hip drive, not arm pull.

**Developing slowly:** Squats the swing (knees bend excessively, hips do not hinge). Pulls the bell with arms. Cannot maintain upright torso in goblet squat.

**Diagnostic questions:** Have you demonstrated the hip hinge drill (wall touch) enough? Most swing problems are hinge problems. If the student cannot hinge without the bell, they cannot swing with it.
